text: double single-port procedure for transanal intersphincteric protectomy and abdominal ileorectal anastomosis resection. This is the case of a forty-year-old woman diagnosed with Crohn's colitis. In two thousand and twelve, after failure of medical treatment, she underwent a laparoscopic subtotal colectomy with ileorectal anastomosis for complicated Crohn's colitis with perianal disease. In years, despite maximal therapy, active rectum disease worsened and she developed an anal-vaginal fistula, needing a loop ileostomy. In two thousand and fifteen, a completion protectomy and definitive ileostomy were ultimately agreed.

The surgeon begins the perineal phase by incising the intersphincteric sulcus and placing a lone star retractor to aid in the dissection of the anal canal. The anus is closed with a Hansen purse string suture to maintain pneumo-rectum. The anal-vaginal fistula is isolated and detached, allowing dissection up to the pelvic floor. A single port device is used to induce pneumorectum, and the gel point path of applied medical is utilized. With pneumorectum at fifteen millimeters of mercury, the surgeon performs a closed rectal dissection using standard laparoscopic instruments, carefully avoiding nerve or adjacent structure injuries until reaching the peritoneal reflection.

During the perineal dissection, the surgeon simultaneously closes the loop ileostomy and abandons it intraperitoneally. The excess tissue is utilized for the single-port gel point. Intraabdominally, a pneumoperitoneum is established at fifteen millimeters of mercury, mirroring the pressure used in the rectum phase.
In the abdominal cavity, the surgeon performs adhesiolysis to mobilize the iliorectal anastomosis. The ileum is resected proximal to the anastomosis using an endogea stapler.
The surgeon performs a transperineal extraction of the specimen, ensuring sufficient length of the last ileum loop to establish a new terminal ileostomy.
